title: Emergency Guide: Cat or Kitten Seizures and Convulsions
meta_desc: If your cat is having a seizure, it is a medical emergency. Learn what to do, why it happens, and how to help your veterinarian triage the situation.
content_html:
TL;DR: Seizures in cats are high-urgency medical emergencies that require immediate veterinary attention, especially if they last more than five minutes. Capturing a video of the episode is highly recommended to help your vet quickly diagnose the cause and begin treatment.

What does a seizure or convulsion look like in my cat?

  • Sudden loss of consciousness
  • Uncontrolled muscle contractions
  • Paddling of the limbs
  • Foaming at the mouth

While frightening to witness, understanding that this is a temporary neurological event is the first step in helping your pet.

Is my cat's seizure a medical emergency?

Yes. Seizures in felines are high-urgency emergencies that can indicate severe issues such as:

  • Toxin ingestion
  • Metabolic imbalance
  • Head trauma
  • Infectious disease
  • Status epilepticus (if the seizure lasts over five minutes or occurs in a row)

You should contact an emergency veterinarian immediately.

Why should I provide video or photo documentation to the triage team?

If you can safely do so, recording a video is incredibly helpful because:

  • Pets often stop seizing by the time they arrive at the clinic.
  • The veterinarian can observe the type, duration, and severity of the convulsion.
  • Visual evidence helps differentiate a true seizure from fainting or vestibular disease.
  • It leads to a faster and more accurate diagnosis during triage.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

In cats and kittens experiencing seizures or convulsions, emergency treatment may include intravenous diazepam boluses (0.5-2 mg/kg), repeated up to three times at 5-10 minute intervals. If seizures persist, a constant-rate infusion of diazepam (0.5-2 mg/kg/hr) may be administered. Alternatively, propofol can be given as a constant rate infusion (0.1-0.6 mg/kg/min), potentially followed by a loading dose of phenobarbital (2-4 mg/kg IV every 6 hours for four doses) if the animal is not already receiving it. In cases where phenobarbital is contraindicated due to hepatic issues, levetiracetam (40-60 mg/kg IV, SC, or rectally) may be considered. Oral anticonvulsants should be resumed as soon as possible if the animal was previously receiving them.

Chapter: Neurology, Emergency, Pharmacology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1220)

faqs.question: What should I do while my cat is seizing?
faqs.answer: Keep your hands away from your cat's mouth to avoid accidental bites. Clear away any sharp objects or furniture they might hit, and keep the environment quiet and dark until you can get them to the vet.

faqs.question: Are seizures in kittens different than in adult cats?
faqs.answer: In kittens, seizures are frequently caused by low blood sugar (hypoglycemia), parasites, or infections, whereas in older cats, they may be linked to organ failure or brain tumors.

faqs.question: Can I give my cat human medication to stop the seizure?
faqs.answer: No. Never administer human medications to a seizing cat. Only a veterinarian should provide medications to stop or manage seizure activity.
